coredata.dc:description: With growing interest, in the industry of carbon nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have massively been explored. It is a potential approach for immense areas of biomedical usage including therapy, imaging, and drug delivery, where CNDs may act as contrast agents, to assess biodistribution and biocompatibility before clinical setting. CNDs offer several advantages over other carbon nanomaterials including better biocompatibility, less toxicity, environment-friendly and many others, also photophysical and chemical properties of CNDs could be altered including their size and shape making them more desirable, furnishing with enhanced biocompatibility and targeting potency for the improvisation of selectivity and sensitivity which may lead to expansion of applications. Besides, carbon nanomaterials could be designed to approach therapeutics innovations and to help or target drugs effectively. This paper addresses carbon nanodots along with its types, properties, different methods of preparation, and treatment of diseases. This review has also highlighted the cutting-edge progress of CNDs in drug delivery as well as its emerging applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Tabulation of different techniques used for the characterization of carbon nanodots.

row.entry: The microscopic techniques used for the characterization of carbon dots are described below [208–210].
Transmission electron microscope is used characterization of the shape and size of the C-dots. Recently, high resolution TEM (HRTEM) is used to studying the lattice and surface imperfections of CDs.
It is high-resolution scanning probe microscopic method.Here, a topographical image of the sample surface can be obtained depending on the interactions between a tip and sample surface.

Using SEM, the sample of CDs are exposed to the high-energy electron beam, the resultant accumulation of charge produces an image which retrieves information regarding their chemical composition, morphology, granular orientation, topography, etc.
Characterization of carbon dots using mass spectrometric methods are described below [208,211,212].
Electrospray Ionization Quadrupole Time-Of-Flight Tandem Mass Spectrometry
ESIQ-TOF-MS/MS includes the use of sensitive MS detection and uses soft ionization method.
Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization Time-Of-Flight Mass Spectrometry

MALDI-TOF-MS, a soft ionization method, is used to characterize the structure of CDs. Here, the mass of ions is determined via the difference in flight times. The ions with the same energy levels are directed towards the detector.
The spectroscopic methods for the characterization of carbon dots are described below [141,208,209,213–222].
IR is a technique for characterization of the chemical functionalities of CDs.It also has the potential to determine the heteroatoms doped on their surface.
PL and UV–Vis spectroscopies are used to study the optical characteristics of CDs, Moreover, these two spectroscopies can be used to calculate the quantum yield (QY) of CDs.

It is one of the most frequently applied, non-destructive and a non-invasive
spectroscopic technique. It is employed for the determination of the carbon state in samples of CDs.

NMR spectroscopy provides additional and the important structural information for CDs. It allows the recognition of the elemental composition, chemical bond formations, and the functional groups present on the surface of CDs.
The characterisation of carbon dots by diffraction techniques is done by following method [209].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is employed for the determination of the crystalline structure, phase purity, and particle size of CDs. These properties can be determined rapidly depending upon their diffraction patterns.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodots as a remedial nanovesicles for drug delivery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With growing interest, in the industry of carbon nanomaterials, carbon nanodots (CNDs) have massively been explored. It is a potential approach for immense areas of biomedical usage including therapy, imaging, and drug delivery, where CNDs may act as contrast agents, to assess biodistribution and biocompatibility before clinical setting. CNDs offer several advantages over other carbon nanomaterials including better biocompatibility, less toxicity, environment-friendly and many others, also photophysical and chemical properties of CNDs could be altered including their size and shape making them more desirable, furnishing with enhanced biocompatibility and targeting potency for the improvisation of selectivity and sensitivity which may lead to expansion of applications. Besides, carbon nanomaterials could be designed to approach therapeutics innovations and to help or target drugs effectively. This paper addresses carbon nanodots along with its types, properties, different methods of preparation, and treatment of diseases. This review has also highlighted the cutting-edge progress of CNDs in drug delivery as well as its emerging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The field of nanotechnology has gained enough limelight as it opens a gateway that is not capable of being accomplished when in bulk state, hence it is rising as an interesting field of research. Nanoparticle; a product of nanotechnology has appealing properties as well as applications [1–6]. Carbon is an element that is biocompatible and has insignificant cytotoxic characteristics, unlike other elements, carbon nanomaterials have brighter chances, but it still needs to be investigated. One of the carbon nanoparticles is carbon nanodots [7].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs) or simply carbon dots (CDs) are carbonaceous materials, derived from organic compounds as shown in Fig. 1 . They are seen as a protagonist as they have become the center of attraction in the field of nanoscience since they were discovered in 2004. Carbon nanodots came into existence as a by-product of the electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Xu et al [8] . These CNDs have a size of less than 10 nm, they are quasi-spherical in shape. Despite of accidental discovery of CNDs, they are gaining tremendous attention as a novel fluorescent nanoparticle that is quantum-sized [9,10]. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) measurement states the outer part of CDs consists of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms and the inner parts usually constitute sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, while the shell consists of organic functional groups such as –CN, –OH, –NH, CO, etc.[11]. The classification of CDs as per the variations in the quantum confinement and crystal structure can be like graphene quantum dots (GQDs), CNDs, and Carbon Quantum dots (CQDs) [12]. CDs are less toxic to cells, compared to metallic Quantum dots, since they are more biocompatible and have higher stability due to their hydrophilic nature [13].

In 2004 during the electrophoretic purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes Xu et al., discovered the fluorescent part which was demonstrated to be carbon nanomaterial by delineation of atomic force microscopy (AFM) [14,15]. Later in 2006 Sun et al., procured high-quantum yield carbon dots from graphite powder and cement by a laser ablation technique which furnished a constructive path to intensify the luminescence of carbon dots [16]. Until the existence of fluorescent carbon nanodots, it was not even expected that carbon could emit light, it was considered as a black material but after the discovery of CNDs, it became desirable due to their strong luminescence and other benign properties [17]. Properties like excellent biocompatibility, increased chemical stability, less toxicity, rich probability for surface functionalization as well as doping, stability in aqueous media, making it extremely utilitarian from the biological point of view, excellent conductivity, viable synthesis from organic materials, photostability, environmental-friendly, high quantum yield. These properties make the CDs advantageous over other well-known quantum dots or QDs like metal oxides, inorganic QDs, and GQDs. The properties and characteristics possessed by carbon nanodots are utilized for numerous applications, like nanocarriers for the delivery of genes, biolabeling, biomedicine, imaging, sensing (which is due to their unique property of luminescence), photodynamic therapy, conversion of photocatalytic energy, drug delivery as a result of surface engineering. The chemical and photophysical properties of CDs can be altered by adjusting their shapes and size, and also by doping with heteroatoms like Sulphur and oxygen [18–22]. Notably, carboxyl, amino, and other groups on the surface of carbon dots extricate additional modifications, which enhance their biocompatibility, and optical competences, targeting the potential as to improve selective and sensitive properties and expand of wide range of applications [23].

Carbon dot is an extensive term used for several nanoscale carbon matter. In an extensive scenario, all nanoscale materials which are mainly made up of carbon can be categorized as carbon dots [24]. Approx. size of a Carbon dot is smaller than ≈10 nm and always exhibits a minimum of one dimension. The inner part of CDs is mostly constituted of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms, although the outer part has sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [25]. Carbon dots mainly consist of graphene quantum dots, carbon nano dots, carbon quantum dots, and carbon polymer dots. Graphene quantum dots are particularly tiny fragments of graphene comprising of one or few-layer sheets of graphene and chemical groups are attached on margins and exhibit anisotropy, with their lateral dimensions exceeding their height. Graphene quantum dots manifest confinement and edge effect and the usual size of a GQD could be less than 20 nm [26]. CQDs always have an obvious crystal lattice structure, manifesting quantum confinement effect and intrinsic state fluorescence. Regulating the size of carbon quantum dots holds significant implications for controlling the PL (photoluminescence) wavelength [27]. Carbon nano dots are amorphous and quasi-spherical in nature and they don’t manifest quantum confinement [28,29]. CNDs possess a variety of functional groups like epoxide, carboxylate, hydroxyl, etc. at the surface [30]. CNDs generally do not show polymer features and obvious crystal lattices, but they manifest a degree of high carbonization. Polymer dots (PDs) possess high luminescence and consist of pi-conjugated polymers and aggregated polymers or cross-linked polymers with less than 100 nm average particle size, which consist of separate and discontinuous medium in water or other liquids with lower molecular weight as continuous phase. PDs are synthesized by monomers or linear polymers [31]. Moreover, PDs can be formed by self-assembling of carbon core and attached chains of polymer. The photoluminescence (PL) in PDs mainly emerges from the crosslink enhanced emission (CEE) effect [24,19,24,33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods of preparation of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
In the present time, numerous synthetic techniques have been discovered for the preparation of carbon dots. To obtain carbon dots with high quality, most studies follow simple, cost-efficient, size-regulating or extensive synthetic methods. The synthetic techniques are classified into physical and chemical methods because of characteristics of the changes of carbon sources to final output as shown in Fig. 2 . Apart from, on consideration of sources and product relationships, two main groups of top-down and bottom-up may recapitulate contemporary synthetic techniques of carbon dots [11]. Commonly, CNDs can be synthesized by two methods: the top-down method and the bottom-up method [34]. In the top-down method, there is a shattering of large carbon structures into carbon nanomaterial by arc discharge, plasma treatment, laser ablation, and electrochemical methods as shown in Fig. 3
[23]. However, this method has many limitations like reaction taking a lot of time, the necessity of costly materials, and stringent reaction conditions [35]. In the bottom-up method, there is a changing of smaller carbon materials into carbon dots of preferred size, this method contains ultrasonic and hydrothermal treatment, carbonization process, pyrolysis, microwave synthesis, and thermal decomposition for manufacturing carbon dots [16]. During the manufacturing process of carbon dots surface passivation and hetero-atom doping are done to ameliorate optical characteristics like photostability, quantum yield, emission wavelength, and many more [36]. The selection of the manufacturing process depends on demand and applications as different kinds of carbon dots show different and contrary properties.

In Top-Down approaches high dimensional bulk carbon nanomaterials are exfoliated to carbon dots having dimensions below 10 nm. And precursors used are carbon nanotubes and graphite, these precursors are exposed to an effective heating environment by several methods like electrochemical approach, chemical oxidation, laser ablation, and arc discharge. Generally, carbon dots that are obtained from these types of methods are not instantly luminating. However, stabilizing their surfaces with numerous polar moieties after synthesizing converts them into high-laminating carbon dots [37,38].

In the electrochemical approach, bulky molecular matter like carbon fiber, graphite, and carbon nanotubes are used to synthesize pure carbon dots by electrolytic activity, in which big organic molecules act as electrodes in actual electrolyte presence. Zhou et al., manifested first electrochemical synthesis of carbon dots by using multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) as an electrode during the process of synthesis. They used precursors with structures of graphite which synthesized graphitic carbon dots or graphitic quantum dots with high blue fluorescence [37].

Zheng et al. used graphite as an electrode in phosphate buffer’s presence at neutral PH conditions to produce pure carbon dots which are soluble in water. The synthesized carbon dots are auspiciously used as potential biosensors [39]. Li et al., also used an electrochemical method to prepare carbon dots but used graphite as both electrodes (cathode and anode). Li et al. and co-workers manifested that for the production of carbon dots, the nature of electrolytes plays a crucial role. When the nature of the electrolyte was alkalescent, the synthesis of carbon dots was successful but when the nature of the electrolyte was acidic carbon dots could not be formed. [40].

The origin of the preparation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by arc discharge method was in 2004 by Xu et al. During the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes by oxidation procedure between arc-discharged soot and nitric acid. Xu et al. accidentally obtained nanoparticles of three types with distinct luminescence properties and relative molecular masses. The obtained CQDs can emit orange, bluish-green, and yellow luminescence. The CQDs produced by this procedure have fine water-soluble properties [14]. Besides, Arora and Sharma specified that Arc Discharge can be used to reorganize carbon atoms which are produced by decomposing bulk carbon precursors in an anodic electrode which is driven via gas plasma achieved in a sealed reactor. Therefore temperature in the reactor can rise to 4,000 K with electric current in concern of production of high energy plasma [41]. The carbon nanoparticle yield obtained in an arc discharge was exceedingly low, furthermore, the arc discharge soot possesses a diverse range of complex components, posing challenges for purification.

The laser ablation technique has been found extensive in creating carbon dots of diverse dimensions. In the laser ablation technique, a pulsed laser beam is used to expose complex organic polymers, resulting in the detachment of nanoscale carbon particles from the macromolecular structure affected by the laser. In 2006 Sun et al. first stated synthesis of carbon dots from graphite powder by laser ablation method [19].

In the laser ablation method three steps are involved, firstly by pulsed laser high energy is absorbed by the carbon substance; followed by thermionic and photoelectric emission electrons are emitted from atoms; and lastly by action of potent force between solid material and positive ions that is repulsive which is produced by high electric field, results in shattering of CDs as shown in Fig. 4 A [42].

Sun et al. synthesized carbon quantum dots by laser ablation within a carbon macromolecule in the presence of carrier gas (argon and water vapors) at 900 centigrade and 75 kPa. Reflux of Nitric acid was employed for 720 min and after that surface was restrained utilizing simplified organic specimen like poly-(propionyl ethylene eimine-coethyleneimine) along with (PEG1500N) resulting in the synthesis of acid-processed carbon quantum dots with fluorescence property [43]. Yu et al. synthesized carbon dots and used toluene as a complex carbon macromolecule by laser ablation technique. The size of carbon dots is regulated via a laser furnace [44]. Hu et al. prepared carbon dots via laser ablation by using carbon powder suspension in Polyethylene glycol (PEG200N) solvent which concurrently plays a role as a surface passivating agent. Thus prepared carbon dots contain many polymer chains at the surface which show a strong emission of the blue color [45].

Ultrasonic treatment is one of the very suitable processes for the synthesis of carbon dots. Robust ultrasonic vibrations give action by breaking large carbon particles[46]. Wang et al. prepared N-doped carbon dots with ammonia and ascorbic acid by ultrasonic treatment [47]. Dang et al. used oligomer-polyamide resin as a carbon macromolecule for the synthesis of carbon dots using one-phase ultrasonic treatment, these carbon dots were low crystalline, presence of functional groups on the surface and evenly dispersed. Also, these carbon dots showed luminescence of white color having a quantum yield of ∼ 28 % [48].

Li et al., synthesized carbon quantum dots having a size limit within 5 nm via ultrasonic treatment using glucose as a carbon precursor. In the first step, by using deionized water they prepared glucose (1 mol/mL) and further added 50 ml HCL into glucose and mixed it. Thereafter, for 4 h the mixed solution went through ultrasonic treatment. Then the prepared solution is dried for 6 h in an oven at 80 °C, and prepared CQDs show bright photoluminescence covering the range from visible to near-infrared spectrum [49].

Ma et al. prepared nitrogen-carbon dots via simple ultrasonic treatment by using glucose and ammonia as starting materials, prepared nitrogen-carbon dots to possess high-up conversion photoluminescence, strong emissions of visible range, and high aqueous dispersibility without surface modifications, simultaneously [50].

Bottom-up approaches are dependent on numerous chemical reactions, leading to the transformation of small carbon sources into nanosized carbon dots. In Bottom-up approaches, carbon dots are synthesized by carbonization of different molecular precursors via microwave-assisted technique, hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal, or pyrolyzation [51,52]. In recent times, interest in the synthesis of carbon dots by bottom-up techniques has increased due to simple execution techniques, economical, accurate management of molecular precursors, convenience, and practicality of utilizing non-toxic precursors in the procedure [53].

Pyrolyzation is a simplistic process for the preparation of carbon dots from molecular precursors via facile chemical reactions achieved at intense temperatures when exposed to strong alkali or acid [54]. Guo et al., prepared steady carbon dots via single-step pyrolyzation of hair (keratin) at 392° Fahrenheit for the reaction time of 1440 min. They achieved successful retrieval of carbon dots and employed it in the detection of mercury (II) ions with enhanced selectivity and sensitivity [55].

In molten lithium nitrate with Ar (Argon) as protecting gas Fu Wang et al., prepared carbon dots via pyrolyzation of citric acid. Alongside citric acid salts, natural things (like green tea, paprika, hair, etc.), synthetic polymers (like epoxy-functional polystyrene), small molecules (like EDTA salts), etc. have also been used to prepare carbon dots via thermal pyrolyzation method [56].

Martindale et al., prepared carbon dots by the pyrolytic approach of citric acid at a high temperature of 180 °C, the prepared CDs possess an average diameter size of ∼ 6 nm and were used in generating hydrogen fuel utilizing solar energy [57]. By directly carbonizing the EDTA (Ethylene Diamine Tetra-acetic Acid) while maintaining a temperature of 260–280 °C via a sand bath, Ma et al., synthesized graphene quantum dots which are N-doped [58].

For the preparation of carbon dots, Yang’s group revealed and manifested microwave-assisted technique. Microwave assisted technique has multiple positive aspects including time-efficient, unvarying heating, inexpensive etc. Concisely, deionized water was used as a medium to dissolve sach Microwave-assistedarides (like fructose, glucose) and polyethylene glycol (PEG200), resulting in a transparent liquid mixture. The solution is heated with the help of microwaves by using a microwave oven for 2–10 min, at 500 W power. Once the transparent liquid mixture was transformed to a mixture of dark brown colour, carbon dots are prepared. Depending upon the time of reaction, carbon dots illustrate contrasting size of average diameters for example 3.65 ± 0.6 nm by heating 10 min, 2.57 ± 0.45 nm by heating for 5 min. Carbon and oxygen were the main components of obtained carbon dots, including high hydrophilic functional groups that enhance the water solubility of carbon dots and allow CDs for biomedical applications [59].

In recent times, Liu et al., employed a microwave-assisted technique for the preparation of carbon dots using precursors like glutaraldehyde and polyethylenimine. Carbon dots with an adjustable colour range from blue to yellow were prepared by altering the molar ratio of glutaraldehyde to polyethylenimine [60]. Kiran et al., prepared carbon dots by using precursors like 3-aminophenyl boronic acid as passivating material and for carbon source citric acid was used. They demonstrated this synthesis by heating both precursors for 4 min in a microwave oven of 1200 W and procuring carbon dots possess average diameter of 2 to 5 nm [61]. Wang et al., used ashes of eggshell membrane as a precursor for synthesizing carbon dots via microwave-assisted technique [62].

The thermal decomposition method is an example of a bottom-up approach for preparing carbon dots. In general, reactions involved in the thermal decomposition approach are endothermic in nature. Generally, a substance is chemically decomposed by the application of heat in this method. Whether, these types of reactions could be reversible (thermal decomposition of limestone, ammonium chloride) as well as irreversible (thermal decomposition of protein, starch) in nature. This method possesses a lot of benefits like cost effectiveness, time efficiency, simplicity of use etc. [35].

Bourlinos et al., stated first thermal decomposition for the synthesis of carbon dots. They used two different starting materials (precursors) for the preparation of two different kinds of CDs. One sample was obtained which was hydrophilic in nature, and by carbonizing salt of octadecyl ammonium citrate, another sample is obtained which is organophilic in nature. Both the samples possess emission properties like photoluminescence tunability [63].

Wang et al., prepared carbon dots via the thermal decomposition method by using citric acid as a precursor. On a hot plate, citric acid was heated for 30 min at a temperature of 200 °C; after that, by using a solution of sodium hydroxide it was neutralized, and later dialysis was done to purify it. The obtained CDs possess sizes within the range of 0.7 nm to 1 nm. Relying on various preparation conditions, prepared CDs stated various quality yields [64].

Dager et al., stated preparation of mono-dispersed carbon quantum dots by using seeds of fennel (Foeniculum vulgare) as a precursor by one-step thermal decomposition, prepared CQDs possess notable PH stability, photostability and no additional surface passivating procedure was required in order to enhance their photoluminescence as represented in Fig. 4B [65].

Hydrothermal synthesis is an admired procedure for synthesizing carbon dots due to clear-cut synthesis method, cost-effective precursors, innocuous, environment-conscious method, and independence of large apparatus. Carbon sources are obtained from carbohydrates, waste peels or organic acids. Because of environmental consciousness, cost-effectiveness, and expanded availability in the last few years, natural carbon sources are preferred over conventional sources for synthesizing carbon dots [66–68]. In general, for synthesizing CDs by this approach, a hydrothermal synthetic reactor is needed for sealing and reacting organic precursors at excessive pressure, intense temperature and for prolonged reaction times [69].

The hydrothermal approach for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots encompasses the processes of dehydrating, polymerizing, carbonization and passivating of starting material. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the starting material plays an important role in the creation of carbon skeletons and the surface functioning of carbon quantum dots, leading to the emergence of highly intense fluorescent properties [70].

Shen et al., prepared carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal synthesis approach by utilizing citric acid and glucose as precursors. Subsequently, coupling of the synthesized carbon quantum dots is done with P25 in order to obtain carbon-quantum-dots/titanium-dioxide composite via hydrothermal synthesis approach for 6 h at 302 degrees Fahrenheit [71].

Mohapatra et al., synthesized highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots having 26 % QY within a single step via a hydrothermal approach using orange juice as a precursor. The prepared carbon quantum dots have an average size of 1.5–4.5 nm and because of their minor toxicity and better photostability, have applications in bioimaging [72].

Pang et al., stated the preparation of nitrogen and Sulphur co-doped carbon dots (NS-CDs) by using methionine as a precursor via a hydrothermal approach. The prepared NS-CDs demonstrate the ability to selectively detect heavy metal ions present in water [73,74,75].

D’souza et al., stated the preparation of carbon quantum dots via hydrothermal synthesis by using slices of carrots as a precursor and then heating it in an autoclave for 12 h at 170 °C. Further dialysis treatment carbon quantum dots were obtained as shown in Fig. 4C [76].

The traditional approaches for the preparation of carbon dots are unjustifiable and inefficient due to the need for modification required post-preparation, expensiveness, lengthy procedures and laborious experimental protocols. Ergo, there is a need to design environment-friendly, cost-effective, non-tedious and more productive methodologies for the preparation of carbon nanodots. In recent years green synthesis has been embraced for the preparation of carbon dots by natural raw material. Natural things like sweet potatoes, shrimp, carbohydrates (like glucose, starch), honey, apple juice, bamboo leaves, green tea, aloe vera, garlic, plant wastes (like willow bark, pomelo peel) etc. were used to obtain carbon precursors which can be used in synthesis of promising luminescent and environment-friendly carbon nanodots [17,65,77–82].

In green synthesis, generally solvothermal/ hydrothermal, microwave-assisted (bottom-up) approaches are used for polymerization and carbonization of natural raw materials as carbon precursors in order to obtain carbon dots. The efficiency of the preparation of carbon dots depends on different routes. In hydrothermal approach carbonization of natural precursors is directly done in order to obtain carbon dots, It is a simple method but several hours are consumed in reaction time. In contrast, the microwave-assisted approach provides consistent, uniform and effective heat, resulting in little reaction time and a quick rate of reaction. Ergo, it has been contemplated as a time-efficient, uncomplicated approach within other approaches, therefore it is widely used for the synthesis of carbon dots [78,83].

In a green approach, banana peels were cleaned with acetone and water, and desiccated at room temp. and carbonization is done in an oven for 12 h at 80 °C, after carbonization banana peels were turned into fine particles of powder and then dispersed in deionized water and put in a microwave for 5 min at 700 W power. After some filtration procedures carbon dots were prepared [84]. A green approach was stated by Bano et al., where a facile single-step hydrothermal approach was used to prepare CQDs of size 260–400 nm by using leaves of Tamarindus indica as carbon source, these biologically compatible QCDs can possibly utilized in biosensing, disease detection, bio-imaging [85]. This technique was employed to synthesize carbon dots by using green tea via a hydrothermal approach at 300 °C for 2 h. These four successive processes: dehydrating, polymerizing, carbonizing and passivating occurred and black carbonized power was obtained which was resuspended deionized water and subjugated to dialysis furthermore for purification of carbon dots as represented in Fig. 4D [86].

In another green approach formation of carbon dots was stated by using orange waste peels as a carbon source via hydrothermal approach, First peels were washed and dried in sunlight and further dried in an oven for 10 h at 150 °C. Then sulfuric acid was added to wash the water-rinsed carbonized peels, drenched with sodium hypochlorite for the time of 4 h, and then washed by using water till the PH of the water reached 7, then it was hydrothermally heated in an autoclave (Teflon lined) at 180 °C for the duration of 12 h. The prepared solution was mixed with dichloromethane and centrifugation was done for 15 min at 5000 rpm to get the solution of brown colour containing carbon dots having a diameter of 2–7 nm with QY 12.3 % as shown in Fig. 4E [87]. The summery of the synthesis approaches for carbon nanodots is tabulated in the Table 1 .

Carbon nanodots have a variety of physiochemical and optical properties, these properties provide a wide variety of applications according to the need. Various physicochemical properties including morphology, size, and luminescence are acquired on the basis of the method of synthesis and precursor being used [88]. The various physiochemical properties are shown in Fig. 5 A.

CDs possess different absorption behaviors according to the carbon sources they are prepared from and the methods used to prepare them. Anyhow, the absorption exhibited by them in the ultraviolet (UV) region (260–320 nm) is strong and the tail extends into the visible range. The CDs derived from tea, have the property of exhibiting strong UV/Vis absorption peaks at 273 nm. The reason behind this is π–π* transition, these transitions are of C = C bands which are of localized clusters of sp2 and the shoulder owing to n–π* transition of carboxyl group’s C = O bands is at 298 nm. The carbon dots which are prepared through top-down methods usually have absorption properties depending upon their sizes. These range from 6.20 to 4.92 eV (200–252 nm) when the size increases from 12 to 22 nm. Moreover, through numerous surface passivation or functionalization techniques, the absorption of C-dots can be decided and modulated. For instance, when C-dot is functionalized with amino groups i.e., ammonia or TTDDA, an exhibition of red shift is observed. In addition to this, a red shift is also noticed when C-dots are synthesized with molecular precursors that are well-defined. For example, CD derived from conjugated polymer, UV/Vis exhibited a broad absorption range, ranging from 400 to 750 nm [89,90,29].

The absorption wavelength that might increase along with emission wavelength due to doping and surface passivation, is a common event with CDs having multi-emission. To give an example, S synthesized via the sun, GQDs co-doped N having 2 absorption bands one at 550 nm and one at 595 nm ascribed to the n- π* and π-π* of S = O and C = S related to Sulphur which was doped. The strong emissions are in general due to trapping of excited-state energy by posterior absorption through surface states [91]. From D-cysteine and L-cysteine, Nie et al. prepared CDs that are immensely fluorescent chiral in nature. The UV/Vis spectrum of the luminescent carbon dots shows absorption bands at two wavelengths, one at 243 nm (aromatic sp2 domain; π-π* transition) and another at 300 nm (CO/ CN/CS due to their n-π* transition), they together possess a weak band at a wavelength of 400 nm. On the contrary, for D-cysteine, no absorption band could be observed above a wavelength of 240 nm. Besides, an observation was also made about the pH-dependent UV/vis pattern of absorption possessed by L-CDs [92].

CDs are known to have an impact on the natural surroundings, mostly on the aquatic flora and fauna and living organisms. So, it makes it important to study its potential influences as we know that it cannot cease to be released into the environment during its preparation, applications, and disposal. To test the impact of CDs, various organisms like aquatic zebra, cell bacterial, rats, and mice were used which showed the outstanding biocompatibility of CDs. Pathological changes were not observed post-entry to CDs into organisms and their elimination via normal metabolic processes was from the body of organisms like in rats it was seen. The biocompatibility properties of CDs aren’t just confined to animals, but also in plants, their growth and resistance to biotic and abiotic stresses, when used judiciously at optimum concentration, have positive effects [93]. The biocompatibility profile of CDs is one of the reasons for its various applications, one of the examples is the drug or gene delivery [94].

Morphological properties of CDs are known to be characterized by using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) [95]. The size of carbon dots based upon the synthesis conditions and the use of precursors ranges from 1 to 10 nm. For instance, the CDs synthesized by natural gas burner’s combustion soot were of size 4.8 ± 0.6 nm and those CDs prepared employing electrochemical oxidations of carbon fibers were found to have a sphere shape. Moreover, for the synthesis of carbon dots having a size of less than 5 nm, pyrolysis of natural products is done. Natural products like coffee, tobacco, eggshell membrane, potato, green tea, pomelo peel, amino acid, etc. CDs synthesized from these products not only influence their size but also their shape. For example, carbon dots synthesized from glycine and grounded coffee were found to be crystalline and mono-dispersed [13]. The crystalline property of carbon dots can be studied and determined by the use of 10 Raman spectroscopy (a rapid, non-destruction-causing tool with high resolution), X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns, and by studying the observations after using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) [96,91,97].

On the other hand, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is used to find out the functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, and, also to determine the elemental composition of CDs. For example, the C1s spectra of carbon dots produced by hydrothermal methods depict 3 kinds of C bonds (carbon bonds), like C–C bond (284.5 eV), C-O bond (286.6 eV) and C O bond (287–9 eV). Regarding the weight ratios, it was found that carbon dots synthesized from carbs like sweet potatoes, their surfaces have carbonyl and hydroxyl groups and their C:O ratio of weight was comparatively less than other naturally synthesized products. The C-dots produced from amino acids had a much higher C: N: O weight ratio when compared with the C-dots produced using flour. From this one conclusion could be drawn that the composition of C-dots was affected by the elemental composition of the precursor [13].

As it was noticed earlier the method of synthesis and precursor used to have a great impact on the size as well as the shape of the carbon dots, generally it is seen that the size of carbon dots is less than 10 nm. A size less than that of 5 nm is regarded as ultrasmall size and the ultrasmall-sized carbon dots have great potential in medical research and development. Due to their small size, they are capable of being filtered by the kidney, post which their elimination occurs from the body; preventing their accumulation in the organs and tissues thereby preventing their toxicity and increasing fame for medical research [98,99,100]. Like the CDs synthesized via microwave-assisted technique by Licciardello et al., through treatment with an aqueous solution of ethylenediamine and L-arginine. PET studies were done in in-vivo systems, it demonstrated speedy renal clearance of carbon dots from the system [101]. Apart from this, the ultrasmall size makes the carbon dots pass the physiological blood–brain barrier (BBB). This was studied by Ji et al., synthesized Mn-doped carbon dots having a size of less than 5 nm by using raw materials such as urea, citric acid, and manganese chloride via microwave method showed the application in the in-vivo magnetic resonance and the in-vitro optical imaging of a mouse brain affected with microglioma, it proved to an efficient T1 nanoprobe as well as possessed a remarkably high potential for cancer imaging agent [102]. Furthermore, the Brownian motion produces aggregation which could be curbed by the generation of sufficient energy by CDs resulting in increased hydrophilicity due to their surface having an oxygen-containing functional group. This also leads to an increment in the aqueous stability as well as the solubility. In addition to that, the ultrasmall-sized CDs can enhance the osmotic pressure of the solution of carbon dots [103].

The solid structure of CDs has shown to have great use in biosensors and chemical sensors, for bioimaging as well as drug delivery, and it was found that most of the CDs have solid structures at present. However, it is suspected that hollow CDs having empty interiors might become more preferred carriers to build drug nano-carrier systems due to various performed studies that portrayed their applications in bio-imaging and anti-neoplastic drug delivery.[15,100,104–106].

Carbon dots have low toxicity at lower concentrations which makes them useful in various applications like bio-imaging, drug delivery via nanotubes for cancer therapeutics and biosensors. However, upon increment in concentration leads to an increment in toxicity for cells, which can lead to the malfunctioning of organs. For instance, it is observed that carbon dots synthesized from graphite by its hydrothermal treatment don’t possess toxicity in the absence of light, however, under light irradiation, toxicity was observed due to the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production [32,33,109].

The cellular toxicity of carbon dots was discovered by Huang et al., cultured AD-293 cells with Carbon dots having different concentration gradients. Viability of more than 100 % and higher activity was observed in AD-293 cells treated with CDs. The in-vitro toxicity of Carbon dots was evaluated by exposure of MCF-7 and HT-29 cells to CDs [110]. On the other hand, for in-vivo evaluation, mice were exposed to CDs. Observation for in-vitro experimentation was that mortality, viability, and proliferation weren’t affected by CDs and that of an in-vivo experiment, when contrasted with a control group that was exposed to 0.9 % NaCl, the group exposed to an aqueous solution had similar body weight increase and normal food intake, studied by Yang et al., [111].

While most of the CDs display observable PL in the dispersed state or solution, some have reportedly shown as aggregates or solid-state PL. AIE property of carbon dots leads to the production of a photosensitizing or photothermal probe and enables LED’s dense packing devoid of impedance. Lately, AIE CDs have been announced due to the diminution of intermolecular movements, ejecting CDs as nano aggregates [112,113].

Not long ago, Hu et al., designed hydrophobic carbon dots (H-CDs), showing AIE via introducing water through the approach of one-pot hydrothermal using melamine (MR) and acetic acid/dithiosalicyclic acid. In dispersed states, blue emission was observed but the introduction of solution with water led to the aggregation with lighting up of red fluorescence [114].

It is found that the synthesis of carbon dots by ultrasonic treatment possesses an up-conversion fluorescence property. Ammonium hydroxide and glucose-synthesized carbon dots showed up-conversion fluorescence in the range 300 to 600 nm and excitation ranging from 650 to 1,000 nm. This property is basically due to the multiphoton process and hence, it is also known as multi-photon PL) referred to as the excitation wavelength which is longer than the emission wavelength, emitted by a single photon when absorption of two or more than two photons takes place. The CDs synthesized by reduction of graphene oxide (GO) by hydrazine hydrate displayed shifting of up-conversion emission peaks from 390 to 468 nm as a result of the change of excitation wavelength from 600 nm to 800 nm. And this spectrum was considered an anti-Stokes transition. Additionally, the up-conversion fluorescence property is beneficial in imaging [115,116,40].

Luminescence is aptly described as an emission of radiation by an atom or molecule, upon returning of these species to the ground state from the excited state. The excitation could be caused by any source and classified accordingly. For example, when the source of excitation is energy that is absorbed from light they are classified as photoluminescence (phosphorescence and fluorescence property), likewise, when energy is a result of reactions that are due to biocatalysis, it is classified as bioluminescence. Similarly, energy is due to chemical reactions, and chemiluminescence [117,118]. Luminescence is classified into the following categories shown in Fig. 5B:

When the source of excitation in CDs is energy due to a chemical reaction it is known to cause chemiluminescence. Besides the excitation process, it is observed that the light emission in PL is similar to the process of emission of light by chemiluminescence (CL) [118,119]. Moreover, CL emission and CL reaction are also seen when there is destruction of electron and hole-injected CQDs [120]. The chemiluminescence property along with carbon quantum dots is used to detect the organic species. For instance, S,-N-CQDs synthesized by hydrothermal methods, monitor indomethacin [121].

Electroluminescence (EL) is also known as electrogenerated chemiluminescence. This is a result of the formation of an excited state by the species produced at the electrodes which encounter elevated energy electron transfer and results in emission of light [122]. Electroluminescence is of great importance and the current research on the fabrication of electroluminescent light-emitting diodes is targeted by great scientists worldwide. EL property investigation by Veca et al., through the integration of bright light-emitting CDs into LEDS having multiple layers portrayed white light and very subtle blue emission which was visible to the naked eyes. This was suggested that CDs having EL is highly likely [123]. The semiconductor nanocrystals show electroluminescence properties leading to the exponential rise in the research and investigation for the ECL to be studied, which might have a use in the field of electrochemistry. A light-emitting diode or LED device based on CQD, it was reported that the color emitted through this device has the potential to be managed by a driving current [34,124]. As a result of high sensitivity, and fewer background signals the carbon dots have shown potential use as a co-reactant and ECL lumonophore to detect various analytes [88].

One of the most attractive properties of CDs is photoluminescence fundamentally and from an application perspective. These have superior light stability, less toxicity and high quantum yield (QY), economical and outstanding superb biocompatibility which make them more advantageous over other fluorescent materials like quantum dots which contain cadmium or lead, organic dyes, etc. QY values quantitatively reflect the brightness of PL which is greatly influenced by the synthesis procedures, the source of carbon, and post-passivation. The CDs synthesized by the “bottom-up” approach have comparatively higher QY than those CDs synthesized by the “top-down” approach, the reason behind higher QY’s of CQDs and CPDs than that of GQDs [125,126]. PL properties of CDs could be assessed by controlling functional groups instead of altering the intrinsic states or core composition. λex-dependent PL properties are also shown by CDs which are slightly different from photoluminescent nanomaterials like silver nanocrystals (NC) or gold nanodots and semiconductor quantum dots. One reason for lex-dependent property could be the size dependency [13].

The excitation wavelength could be controlled to regulate the PL of carbon dots keeping their structure and size the same which aids in the multi-color bioimaging [127]. Excitation-dependent PL is shown by almost all the CDs along strong emission in the region of blue wavelength, decaying rapidly in the region of red wavelength. Great efforts are made in the manipulation of photoluminescent CDs for their predicted application in bio-labelling, bioimaging, biosensing, and drug delivery [108,128].

Phosphorescence of CDs is not usually researched, unlike fluorescence, despite having more advantageous and better properties than fluorescence. For example, larger Stokes shift, high signal-to-noise ratios, and greater lived luminescence, However, it is a fact that it is difficult to attain phosphorescence involving spin-forbidden transitions and radiative pathways between single and triple states as well as intersystem-crossing. The observation of this property in the powdered CDs is hard because of the properties it possesses in the aggregation state, which include self-quenching and deliquescence [129,130].

Prior to 2018, a study on room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) CDs was concentrated mainly on the immobilized or embedded carbon dots in specific matrices such as polyurethane. RTP is considered one of the most fascinating properties of the carbon dots and its production mainly revolves around two processes including intersystem crossing (ISC) from singlet state having the lowest excitation (S1) to triple state (Tn) and radiative transition from triplet state having lowest excitation (T1) to ground state (S0). Henceforth, for production of RTP from organics requires a critical nonradiative decay along with an effective and efficient ISC process. The ISC process could be favored by doping with heteroatoms such as halogens, N, P which can encourage the n − π* transition of C-N and C-O [127]. To date, hardly any RTP characteristics of naked CDs have been reported. However, enough success has been achieved for the production of RTP CDs nowadays, via embedding carbon dots in different matrices. Still, these RTP CDs are at the juvenile stage and need to be improved further in various ways such as adjustment of reaction conditions [127]. A method for the preparation of metal-devoid RTP material is simple and easy through the construction of polymer carbon dots directly using hydrothermal treatment, and there’s no introduction of additional supporting matrix [129]. This property of phosphorescence in the CDs is a relatively new advancement and its application majorly revolves around encrypted information, but it should be expanded rather than being confined to this. There is a potential for future research direction and development of various uses of phosphorescent CDs including their use in bioimaging due to their superior biocompatibility and less toxic characteristics [38].

One of the most attractive features of CDs is fluorescence. It is deduced that the PL character or property of CD is a contribution by radiative recombination of the excitons, emissive traps, and quantum effects. The formation of surface fluorescence is due to a mixture of sizes or different surface states of CDs which indicates that the fluorescence emission is a result of small sizes or surface states. Strong blue to green emission is emitted by almost all CDs, long emission wavelength [orange to red/near-infrared (NIR)] cannot be observed since they are very weak. Due to the scattering of light and autofluorescence of the bio-substrate in the region of short wavelength, the wavelengths emitting CDs have bleak chances to be useful as nanoprobes in bio-medicine. On the other hand, long wavelength emitting CDs mainly the NIR or red fluorescence have a probability of providing deep light penetration and greater imaging contrast inside the specimens. The manufacturing of CDs doped with heteroatoms has been a topic of conversation for the production of long-wavelength emitting CDs. To enhance the structural properties as well as to change the electrical density of carbon dots, they could be doped with different elements whose structure and composition can be altered [115,131–133].

Excitation-independent emission in the CDs co-doped with N, S has been reported, although excitation-dependent fluorescence is shown by most of the CDs. The NIR fluorescent carbon dots have distinctive characteristics like specific uptake at tumors, NIR emission, and great photothermal converting efficiency, which makes it important in advanced applications in photothermal therapy and the NIR fluorescent imaging [134]. Pang et al., produced excitation-independent CDs having tunable fluorescent colors by using a wet oxidative procedure which was well controlled. The conclusion was the determination of the photoluminescent properties of the carbon dots based on the degree of surface oxidation and molecular weight. The excitation-dependent or independent photoluminescence arises from carbon dots’ surface states [135].

The property of dispersibility depends majorly on the surface chemistry or carbon dots and it is an elemental property for the application and solution-processing of CDs as shown in Fig. 5C. The fact that most of the CDs have superior solubility profiles and hydrophilicity, which are due to the functional groups having oxygen, these are produced by synthesizing or obtained from precursors. Nonetheless, the synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dots can be done by using hydrophobic precursors and their limited carbonization along with modification of its surface using molecules possessing hydrophobicity. An example of this is the synthesis of hydrophobic carbon dot by carbonizing the octadecyl ammonia (having molecular formula C18H37NH+) citrate salt in the air dispersed deftly in organic solvents such as hexane, toluene, chloroform, etc. at elevated concentrations of 50 mg/ ml [63,136].

The dispersibility and hydrophilicity properties may have an impact on the probable applications of CDs. For instance, to image tumors, the use of hydrophilic carbon dots as a fluorescent probe on the other hand, the CDs possessing hydrophobicity are used to catalyze numerous reactions in the organic chemistry [137].

There are numerous potential applications for the fascinating family of nanomaterials known as carbon nanodots (CNDs) in fields like nanotechnology, biology, and electronics. Understanding the composition, characteristics,and functionality of CNDs is crucial. To fully understand the physical, chemical, and optical features of CNDs, characterizing them frequently entails combining a variety of approaches. These characterizations are essential to modifying CNDs for specific applications, such as imaging, sensing, drug delivery, etc., and to ensure their quality and reproducibility. Morphological characterization, chemical composition, structural characterization, optical qualities, etc. are some of the several characterizations. The different techniques used for evaluation/ characterization of nanodots are represented in the Table 2 .

Carbon nanodots (CDs) have good biocompatibility, strong photoluminescent effect, photostability, and efficient physicochemical properties as well as they are unaffected by metabolic degradation which makes them beneficial in the advanced development of fluorescent biosensors for the bioimaging [36,138,139]. Early research of carbon nanodots mainly covered its use in cellular imaging but current discoveries have moved the focus to target-specific drug delivery, bioimaging and theragnostic approaches. On the basis of different structures, carbon nanodots are engaged to adapt cell membrane permeability, gene expression and enzyme catalytic activity [138].

Carbon Nanodots are used to establish different sensing system designs apart from its fluorescence on/off method such as bi-modular detection, peak shift, lifetime changes and ratio metric sensing. Carbon Nanodots implant a high-performance micro total analysis system that is highly desirable for crucial detection/ sensing areas like radiation prevention, public safety and early detection of diseases [138]. Fig. 6 A indicates different applications of carbon nanodots.

Carbon nanodots owing to their above-mentioned special mix of properties, make a potential candidate to be employed in various applications [37]. The sharp emitting CDs, when blended with the marked electron-donating capacity can be utilized, for example in optoelectrical devices. The emission that is selective and sensitive to identify metal ions and molecules present in the solution can be manipulated for generating nano sensing devices which can potentially determine various cations [37,140]. Their non-toxic nature makes them beneficial to be applied in various in-vitro and in-vivo bioimaging and drug delivery experiments [37]. However, CDs to be employed as sensors involve a need for a direct correlation between the concentration of the analyte and fluorescence conduct (elevated intensity, quenching or recovery) or else there might not be identifiable signs of the analyte present. Different studies describe that specific types of CDs show sensitivity towards particular chemicals, which can be exploited for diagnosing in a clinical setting of patients having imbalanced levels of, such as protein, metal ion or a specific hormone [141].

The fabrication of different carbon quantum dots through various methods give rise to sharp colors in photoluminescence, starting from deep ultraviolet to blue, green, yellow and red in which blue and green are the most common. Further, CQDs photoluminescence is influenced by a variety of factors like the degree of surface oxidation, surface functionalization, concentration, dimension, shape, wavelength excitation and also N/S doping [139]. CQDs and GQDs are widely analyzed as fluorescent probes for the purpose of determination of heavy metal ions and pesticides. So far, the most extensively researched analytes are metal ions in which CQDs are employed as sensors [142]. Thus, the sensors that have been established for metal ions detection comprises of Fe3 +, Zn2 +, Hg2 +, Ag+, Cu2 +, Pb2 +, K+, Be2 +, Cr6 +, Sn2 +, Al3 +, Pd2 +, Bi3 +, Cd2 +, Au3 +, Mn2 +, Ni2 +, Co2 + [13,22,139]. Many of the studies are based on the notion that metal ions uptake the CQDs luminescence [22]. PL turn-on/off assays employ the PL properties of CDs/GQDs for the identification of different metal ions, and proteins inside the cells of in the solution [143].

Chemical sensors can be used in the identification of different species without any changes in the CQDs/ GQDs preparation. However, CQDs and GQDs essentially need to undergo various adaptations with DNA, RNA, Enzymes, cells, antigens, tissues, and microorganisms to be employed as biosensors. Though the number of CQDs and GQDs used for biosensing is limited, but the recent studies are anticipated to evolve rapidly [144].

Mohammadi and coworkers described a biosensing platform i.e., Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) which employed the energy donor–acceptor pairs, MnO2 nanosheets and CDs for sensitive identification of microRNA. The assay reported the ability to differentiate between the single-base mismatched miRNA-155 and complementary targeted miRNA-155 with higher accuracy. This approach was applied to diagnose breast cancer MCF-7 cells and the doped serum samples [145]. Another FRET biosensor was fabricated by Hamd-Ghadareh and coworkers in which two CDs i.e., one was citric acid derived (CD-1) and the other was resorcinol derived (CD-2). CD-1 and CD-2 were in conjugation with tumor-associated biomarkers CA125 and CA15-3 that were employed as FRET donors while 2D nanosheets of MoS2 provided their concurrent identification as it was used as a nano platform for aptamer molecule adsorption thus, acting as FRET acceptor. This FRET study reported systemic performance for identifying MCF-7 and OVCAR-3 cell lines and specific OVCAR-3 and MCF-7 tumor cell imaging [145]. Correspondingly, Kanamycin is an antibiotic whose residues were identified in the milk samples through a pair of carbon nanodots-MoS2 nanosheets by employing aptamer structural configuration induced FRET within MoS2 layers and CDs. In this process, distance-reliant transfer of energy is bridged by aptamer of single-stranded DNA [146]. Shi et al., combined GQDs with AuNPs for utilizing them to establish FRET sensors for the identification of gene sequences unique to S. aureus. Surface active carboxylic moiety modifies the GQDs with aminated ssDNA on its surface. The thiolated DNA probes adapted AuNPs were also designed. The S. aureus having that specific gene sequence initiates bridging among the sequences of the probes on GQDs and AuNPs respectively to transfer the energy by bringing them close enough for it to happen. The fluorescence quenching efficiency extended up to 87 % of the fluorescence signal after adding targeted oligo (100 nm). Thus, this research shows the capability of well-designed CQDs/GQDs modifications for biosensing applications [22].

The basis of photoelectrochemical biosensors is that photoirradiation can only initiate the transfer of electrons occurring on the electrode. Thus, the CQDs and GQDs owing to their optical properties give enough chance for them to be employed in the fabrication of such biosensors. Photoelectrochemical biosensor which was aptamer based for the identification of zeatin established on the GQDs/graphite-like C3N4 nanocomposites (g-C3N4). GQDs were employed for the purpose of enhancing the photoelectric converting ability of g-C3N4. This biosensor on collaborating with aptamer labeled DNA biotin and AuNPs showcased a low limit of detection of 0.031 nm, 0.1 nm to 100 nm of linear ranges, and incorporation of indole-3-butyric acid, indole-3-propionic acid, and indole-3-acetic acid, gibberellin and 6-benzylaminopurine enhances selectivity [147].

Matrix-assisted laser desorption ionization mass spectrometry (MALDI MS) is an effective instrument for fast detection of biomacromolecules, that is generally carried out with traditional organic matrices. For a faster and higher yield analysis by MALDI MS of small biomolecules, Hydroxyl group-associated carbon nanodots are used as a matrix. Moreover, it facilitates high-performance identification of 1fmol for a variety of small biomolecules with real-time monitoring, in situ imaging, and low noise, offering comprehensive analysis of biological samples. For example, Carbon nanodot matrices can envisage detecting small molecules in conventional Chinese herbs, identifying complex oligosaccharides and also superficial identification of Puerarin in the serum [148]. Carbon nanodots are a perfect example of the matrix for spatiotemporal mapping or imaging of biomolecules of smaller size and/ or their metabolites specifically at the sub-organ tissue level. The efficient results of carbon nanodots employed as a matrix owe to the hydroxyl group attached to the surface or nanodots. Due to the requirement of lesser laser energy for detecting small biomolecules (50 µJ), the carbon nanodots Fe matrix is effective in enhancing laser lifetime and also in improvising spatial resolution in imaging using MALDI MS. Carbon Nanodots matrix used in MALDI MS on storing for two years at room temperature still yields highly efficient results in imaging and detection of smaller biomolecules. Carbon nanodots matrix may discover a new process for visualizing and analyzing small biomolecules by MALDI MS. Furthermore, MALDI MS with carbon nanodots as matrix is propitious for commercialization due to its cheap and mass production capability [149].

The CDs fabricated from citric acid and L-cysteine have higher fluorescence properties through an economic and easy, short-term microwave-assisted approach. CDs were utilized for in vitro imaging as a fluorescent probe by which the CRL-5822 cell line, a common cancer cell and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were studied. According to the study it was found and established that Dopamine solution was responsible for the quenching nature of CDs and also the brightness of fluorescence of CDs directly depended upon the concentration of dopamine in the solution. Thus, the synthesized CDs reported good biocompatibility for both in-vivo and in-vitro bioimaging applications [22].

Bandi and coworkers employed CDs as multicoloured nanoprobes owing to their property of exciting tunable emission through which various excitation wavelengths can be achieved. The researchers produced HeLa cells at 200 mg ml−1 concentration for 6 h, containing CDs in DME medium, and then a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) was used to test the emission of colored fluorescence of CDs in various excitation states. The synthesized CDs showed emissions of red, green and blue colors at 561 nm, 488 nm and 405 nm excitations respectively. On the other hand, controlled cells without CDs when exposed to similar conditions showed no fluorescence [145]. Photostability and resistant properties against photobleaching make CQDs beneficial in the application of bioimaging. One such instance is the fabrication of CQDs by pyrolysis of plastic waste residues utilized in MDA-MB 468 cell line imaging. The intensity fluorescence reported up to 1 h of stability from the cells after persistent excitations. The CQDs demonstrated excellent photostability and lesser photobleaching [22,81].

The mitochondria isolated from cells have also been used in delivering carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for in-vivo imaging, it was studied by Li et al., CQDs-loaded with mitochondria (Mito-CQD) were able to display in-vitro cancer cells images. On the other hand, Near Infrared Fluorescent CQDs were established for in-vivo imaging of animals, and the near-infrared daylight 680 (Dy680) and improvised CQDs were loaded into the isolated mitochondria (Mito-CQD-Dy680) [150].

Zebrafish were significantly used in many crucial biosciences like amelioration of mechanisms, disease expansion, and pattern formation. The use of zebrafish was established due to its explicit developing stages and susceptibility to optical imaging. Thus, zebrafish was chosen for the model of fluorescence imaging through which carbon nanodots were discovered to heap up selectively in the yolk sac and eyes. Moreover, observation of long-term developmental stages of zebrafish carbon nanodots was conservated for over 60 h. In-vivo imaging used PEG-encapsulated carbon nanodots and Zns-doped carbon nanodots (CZns-dots-PEG) regardless of their green and blue emissions, they were not desirable for in-vivo imaging. Further, no evident toxicity was observed in any organs and tissues under various different excitations, red and green emitted fluorescence pictures were observed. The lymph of mice was labelled and carbon nanodots were examined by issuing it through intradermal injection. In comparison, pure carbon nanodots were metabolized more rapidly than PEG incorporated carbon nanodots. After the intravenous administration, carbon nanodots were observed through fluorescence in the stomach and then gathered in the bladder past 1 h. Fluorescence reduction was observed after 4 h of injection and was collected in the kidney which defines the subsequent excretion of carbon nanodots through urine. In-vivo images could be seen of various excitations. Carbon nanodots may get concentrated in the brain and give blue color appearance when they are applied for in-vivo imaging [26,151]. Beyond their uses in in-vivo bioimaging, these CDs have some limitations, such as their lack of in-depthtissue penetration, lack of specificity to target particular cells or molecular compartments, difficulty in quantitatively analyzing in-vivo data using CDs due to the complexity of biological environments and varying fluorescence intensities, and photobleaching of CDs caused by continuous exposure to light, which causes quenching [152]. Despite the promising potential of this technology, transitioning fluorescence imaging from research facilities to clinical applications presents a number of challenges and issues, including regulatory approval, clinical validation—where clinical studies are necessary to validate the use of fluorescence imaging in disease diagnosis and guiding surgical procedures, biocompatibility and safety, cost and accessibility, quantitative accuracy, data management and privacy, and others [153]. Fluorescence imaging has advanced significantly in clinical applications, particularly in oncology and surgery, despite these obstacles. In order to resolve these problems and realize the full potential of fluorescence imaging in clinical practice, collaborations between researchers, physicians, and industry will be essential. These collaborations will also depend on improvements in imaging technology and regulatory frameworks [154].

Initial cancer identification plays a very crucial role in the consequent treatment and diagnosis of carcinoma. Early tumor cell detection at their molecular stage is essential for an inchoate cancer diagnosis. However, the main challenge lies in the differentiation of normal cells from cancer cells. However, it has been observed that normal cells get stimulated by some molecules to go through a complex series of reactions to form cancer cells. Therefore, identifying such molecules in cancerous cells which are overproducing are known as cancer biomarkers which may be a practical and reliable estimate for diagnosing cancer [155,156].

Folate Receptor (FR) is one of the essential cancer biomarkers which is found remarkably excessive in numerous malignant cell epithelium to that in normal cells. These cancerous cells overexpression in humans involve malignancies of the throat, kidney, ovary, breast, lung, prostate and brain. Also, they are minutely present in normal healthy human cells. These folate receptors on the malignant cell surface can be exploited by specifically focusing on the folic acid group of the receptor. The folic acid acts as a propitious ligand with higher affinity and excellent stability for FR and as the cancer advances, FR expression increases accordingly [129,157]. Thus, measuring of FR expression is feasible in differentiating cancer and normal cells. Cells have folic acid incorporated by endocytosis which is receptor-mediated and has an excellent compatibility in both organic and aqueous solvents which possibly prove to be more precise and discriminating cancer diagnostic way. The development of folic acid conjugated with carbon nanodots in which folic acid was linked to the amine-capped nanodots covalently. Hence, folic acid-specific carbon nanodots enter the cancerous cells by exceptionally interacting with FR which was evidently shown in the study by Song and coworkers that carbon nanodots can discriminate cancer cells from normal healthy cells by overexpression of FR for the purpose of targeted imaging [31,158]. The study results showed that greater intensity of fluorescence was observed in HeLa cells than in healthy cells, confirming that cancer cells that are folate receptor positive can be detected through this system. Moreover, carbon nanodots have also been created by conjugation of folic acid on the surface of fluorescent active dry yeast-extracted carbon nanodots by Zhang and coworkers for specific cancer cell imaging. The folic acid carbon nanodots through imaging of fluorescence demonstrated the discrimination of PC12 cells and HepG2 cells that were FR-positive, suggesting that folic acid carbon nanodots are highly promising candidates in the cancer diagnosis [159]. Liu et al., assayed the cancer cells with FR overexpression on their cell surface by using a label-free and common method i.e., photoelectrochemical cyto-sensing. This method circled around the transfer of resonance energy by linking cysteamine-capped gold nanocarriers and carbon nanodots that lead to CD-AuNPs-Cys conjugation providing a remarkable photoelectrochemical property [145].

Glycoproteins are also one of the most used biomarkers for cancer. Glycoproteins particularly present on the surface of cancerous cells are identified by various saccharide molecules. Hence, for targeted cancer cell detection glycosylated nanoparticles are observed as a promising identifying agent. HepG2 cell surface has protein overexpression of mannose receptor and sialyl lewisa receptor. Carbon nanodots encapsulated mannosylated liposomes were created that showed imaging and specific identification of HepG2 cells. Therefore, this probe can effectively establish a system for drug delivery for the concurrent application of targeting receptors and bioimaging [129]. Transferrin which is a serum glycoprotein was modified with PEGylated graphene oxide (GO) that is characterised as a ligand for glioma cell targeting due to their overexpression with respective receptors. Male Sprague-Dawley rats were divided into three separate groups of glioma rats to which Free Doxorubicin and doxorubicin-doped PEGylated GO without and with transferrin were injected respectively. Liu and coworkers reported on the basis of distinct samples of the brain after HPLC analysis displayed Doxorubicin PEGylated GO with transferrin modification in excessive concentrations to that of the other two groups. Also, the volume of tumor in rats was decreased [160,161].

Also, Gao and coworkers studied radical scavenger Glutathione overexpression seen in different tumor cells contrasting them from healthy cells. Silane molecules and glycerol were used to create carbon nanodots via the solvothermal method. However, the fluorescence of CD/Fe3 + is recovered by glutathione because Fe3 + damps out the fluorescence of carbon nanodots. Thus, Glutathione overexpression in tumor cells is identified by CD/Fe3 + as it can easily differentiate it from the normal cells. Moreover, on their IV administration, the site of tumour cells displayed excessive fluorescence, proving CD/Fe3 + as a potential candidate for diagnosis of cancer [162].

According to WHO, glioma is recognized as malignant brain tumors which can be classified from I to IV grades. Gliomas have gained much attention in cancer research due to increase in the deaths of humans. Currently, there is no treatment of gliomas available but many nanoparticles-based approaches are being discovered for the therapeutic treatment of cancer along with the gliomas. Carbon Nanodots has received much popularity within nanoparticles in the diagnosis and treatment of brain tumors due to their high quantum yields and interference is negligible. Carbon nanodots have the ability to cross the blood–brain barrier and reach the CNS effectively which makes them helpful in early detection of brain disorders [163].

Gadolinium (Gd3 +) is a scarce element on earth that reveals exceptional contrasting amplification owing to its paramagnetic properties, it is usually introduced into nanocarriers to impart their magnetic functions. Due to this, Gong and coworkers discovered the synthesis of Gd3 + -loaded CDs via microwave assistance [140156]. Gd3 +-polymer-loaded carbon nanodots have proven to be a promising contender for the imaging of gliomas via targeted MR fluorescence imaging. Polymer-coated carbon nanodots such as the conjugation of Gd3 +- diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) develop a dual model imaging system. They possess magnificent qualities like excessive quantum dots, size uniformity, good dispersity and elevated contrast ability in MRI in in-vitro study and also significant uptake by tumor cells that lead to its successful entrance into the brain by crossing the BBB for detection of tumors with high-resolution imaging [163156].

Another promising carbon nanodot was produced by using a solvothermal method which is a beneficial synthetic approach for the making of polymer coated nitrogen doped carbon nanodots. In this method, nitrogen is doped in carbon nanodots in such a way that the carbon dot core has nitrogen element incorporated into it, which enhances its properties such as desirable dispersibility in water, dynamic cell distribution with its introduction inside the nucleus, higher cellular uptake and tunable fluorescence. Multicolored fluorescence imaging through nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots exhibits excitation which relies on the emission of fluorescence by the carbonization extraction [164].

Near-infrared fluorescence (NIRF) releasing carbon nanodots made from tire soot via oxidation of nitric acid is employed as a near-infrared imaging agent for cell imaging. Even though carbon nanodots possess good penetration ability to cross BBB, it is an advantage to develop a methodical synthetic process which is easy and eco-friendly. Thus, nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots were produced by hydrothermal process from milk which is highly fluorescent. Imaging is done using probes like fabricated carbon nanodots for the detection of human glioma tumor cell lines (U87 Cell). The study of carbon nanodots on cellular uptake resulted in their successful incorporation in U87 cell lines which was identified by the intensity of bright fluorescence in the cells, proving that carbon nanodots can be used in targeted drug delivery as vehicles to reach into cancer cells [165].

Carbon Nanodots are widely accepted as a potential contender for non-invasive imaging of tumors despite their biocompatibility and lesser toxicity. These nanodots are studied for their effectiveness in the imaging of gliomas. In researching through this approach, the synthesis of carbon nanodots was done by taking glycine as a single precursor by simple heating. The prepared carbon nanodots revealed excessive emission at 500 nm. According to in-vivo studies, carbon nanodots displayed high potential in restricting the cells of glioma in the brain instead of normal cells and tissues which proves them to be safe for normal cells and could be a possible competitor in the treatment of brain tumors. Moreover, they have the excessive capability to get concentrated at the site of tumors, recognizing C6 glioma cells through effectual imaging [99].

Designing carbon nanodots comprising distinctive surface chemistry is a highly demanded barrier in the imaging of brain tumors for their specific concentration in the cells of tumor. The Carbon nitride quantum dots (CNQDs) were produced from the derivatives of urea as pre- pre-cursor for the best possible examination of glioma cells in children through their discriminatory entrance into the glioma cells and thus, their subsequent imaging. Significantly, CNQDs do not diffuse inside normal human embryonic kidney cells and specifically invade tumor cells (SJGBM2). CNQDs proved as one of the best possible nanoparticles for drug delivery for cancer therapy and its specific bioimaging. Diffusion studies of CNQDs reveal that they diffused inside lysosomes in the cytoplasm of cells in less than 6 h [166].

Microwave-assisted polyol process was designed for synthesizing folic acid procured CNQDs by utilizing D-glucose, folic acid and L-aspartic acid as a source of carbon. The carbon nanodots derived from these sources displayed magnificent biocompatibility and tunable fluorescence which are exploited for targeted C6 glioma cell imaging without further addition of any extra dye. In-vivo examinations established that Glu-Asp-Carbon Nanodots resulted in prominent distinction of biodistribution in targeted cells in less than 15 min and displayed bright fluorescent signals in C6 tumor cells of glioma comparatively to the normal brain which indicates their distinctive capability to extend up to the targeted C6 glioma tumor cell with great accuracy by readily crossing the BBB [167].

Carbon Nanodots consisting of functionalizing surface and crystalline nature are incorporated as effective components in designing and making mesoporous materials, model catalysts and hybrid composites. Carbon nanodots subunits are assumed to be an economic way of preparing mesoporous carbon nanomaterials and carbon nanodots/ SiO2 nanocomposites. Various different carbon nanodots employed in mesoporous silica particles show applications in drug delivery and bioimaging [138].

Neurodegenerative disorders (NDDs) are characterized by a decrease in memory aptitude and impairment of learning ability. NDD leads to the accretion of amyloid-beta and increases the level of inflammation-causing cytokines. Near-infrared (NIR) probes have been established in recent years for the imaging of cells, a wide range of them having higher sensitivity and resolution are used in the differentiation of healthy cells from diseased cells. Thus, NDDs can be prevented by aiming at the inflammation and amyloid-beta peptide. The potential of restraining the accumulation of amyloid and reduction of pro-inflammatory cytokines like TNF-alpha and IL-1, IL-6, IL-17a, IL-33 is observed by GQDs loaded with glycine-proline-glutamate neuroprotective peptides improve memory ability and learning along with dendritic spine [168–170].

Carbon nanodots are efficiently employed as potential agents in separating the human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) which is an important factor in causing toxicity in the brain. This concept was derived from a study done on embryonic zebrafish, where GQDs resulted in a reduction of hIAPP by exceptionally decreasing the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) that ultimately leads to amyloidosis. As the experimentations prove amyloid beta is a promising agent in Alzheimer's disease (AD) pathogenesis. Functionalized carbon nanodots are effective in therapy for AD as they are capable of crossing the BBB, along with their excessive biocompatibility they inhibit amyloid-beta aggregation as shown in Fig. 6B. Carbon nanodots were synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method in which the source of carbon was ammonium citrate which was later modified by branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI). These bPEI carbon nanodots display a high potential of negatively aiming at amyloid beta accretion and the function of illumination under light was also enhanced [163].
